message:
fix(security): bounded config reads, system symlink rejection, JSONL rotation, 6-digit allow-once codes

Four privilege/safety hardening changes that travel together because
they share `src/config.rs`'s new bounded-read primitive.

## Bounded config-file reads + system-layer symlink rejection (`tck0`)

`config::load_layer_from_file` and `allowlist::load_allowlist_file`
previously called `fs::read_to_string` directly. A 2 GiB symlinked
file (or just an enormous TOML written into a config path) would be
fully loaded into memory before parsing failed.

New `pub(crate) read_config_file_bounded(path, source)` helper in
`src/config.rs`:

- Returns `Option<String>`, `None` on any failure (missing, too large,
  unsafe symlink, non-UTF-8).
- Caps reads at `MAX_CONFIG_BYTES` (1 MiB — well above any sane TOML
  config) using `std::io::Read::take`.
- Tracks per-layer trust class with a new `ConfigSource` enum:
  `System` for `/etc/dcg/config.toml` and `AllowlistLayer::System`,
  `Untrusted` for everything else.
- For `ConfigSource::System` only: if the path is a symlink and the
  symlink target's directory is user-writable (no sticky bit, world-
  writable, or owned by a non-root user), refuse to read. A non-root
  user could otherwise plant a symlink at `/etc/dcg/config.toml`
  pointing at `~/their-config.toml` and influence privileged config.

`load_layer_from_file` and `load_allowlist_file` are switched over to
the new helper. Allowlist callers report a single composite error
("missing, too large, or unsafe symlink") since the helper folds the
distinction behind `Option`.

Four new tests in `src/config.rs`:
`read_config_file_bounded_returns_content_under_cap`,
`...rejects_oversized_file`, `...returns_none_for_missing_path`,
`...system_rejects_user_writable_symlink_target`.

## Pending-exceptions JSONL bounded with rotation (`f81d`)

`record_block` previously appended unbounded. Long-running automations
issuing many allow-once codes turned every call into O(N) under an
exclusive lock as the file grew, then re-read every record on the
next `record_block` for active-set computation.

New caps:

- `MAX_PENDING_LINES = 10_000` — when the active set exceeds this on a
  load, the oldest half is archived to `pending.jsonl.1` and only the
  newest `MAX_PENDING_LINES / 2` records remain in the live file.
  Hysteresis matches typical log-rotation behavior.
- `MAX_PENDING_BYTES = 10 MiB` — hard refusal: if after rotation the
  live file is still over this byte cap (a single record is somehow
  enormous), `record_block` returns an error rather than continuing
  to grow. Failing closed is the safe posture for this code path.
- The archive file is opened with `OpenOptions::create(true).append
  (true)` so prior archives accumulate (operator gets a growing audit
  trail until they manually compact) rather than being overwritten.

Hot path complexity is now O(MAX_PENDING_LINES / 2) under the
exclusive lock instead of unbounded.

## 6-digit allow-once short codes (`suap`)

`short_code_from_hash` was a 5-digit decimal modulo of the trailing
32 bits, giving 100,000 codes and a birthday-paradox 50% collision
threshold at ~370 active records. Bumped to 6 digits (1,000,000
codespace) which raises the collision threshold to ~1,175 active
records — well above realistic per-day volume given the 24-hour TTL.

Compatibility: `dcg allow-once <code>` and `dcg allowlist revoke
<code>` still accept legacy 5-digit codes from already-written
`pending.jsonl` files. The fixture in
`tests/json_schema_conformance.rs` is updated from "12345" to
"123456" so the rehydrated `<DYNAMIC>` allowOnceCode matches the new
length.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
